explanation_md: `report` takes `Base` by value. When `d` is passed, it is sliced: only the `Base` portion is copied into parameter `b`. The vtable pointer in `b` is for `Base`, so `b.id()` dispatches to `Base::id`, printing `"Base"`. Changing the parameter to `const Base&` would fix this.

prompt_md: Why does object slicing happen when a derived object is assigned to a base-class value?

options.a: The base class destructor runs on the derived members during the copy.
options.b: The copy constructor of the base class only copies base-class data members, discarding derived members.
options.c: The compiler cannot determine the runtime type during a value copy.
options.d: Virtual functions are disabled when an object is copied.

explanation_md: When you copy-construct or assign a derived object into a base-class variable, the base class's copy constructor (or copy-assignment operator) runs. It only knows about and copies the base-class data members. Derived members are simply not copied — they are sliced away. The vtable pointer is then set to point at the base vtable, not the derived vtable.

`ref` is a `const Shape&` that binds to `sq`. A reference does not copy the object — it is an alias for the same `Square` in memory. Virtual dispatch follows the vtable of the actual object, which is `Square`'s, so `ref.kind()` returns `"Square"`. This is the correct way to avoid slicing.

prompt_md: Which of the following correctly stores a heterogeneous collection of `Animal`-derived objects without slicing?
explanation_md: Option (a) slices every element to `Animal` on insertion. Option (b) is ill-formed — standard containers cannot store references. Option (d) is a plain array of `Animal` values, which also slices. Option (c) stores pointers; the actual `Dog` or `Cat` objects live elsewhere and are not copied, so their derived data and vtable pointers are preserved.
